coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of iron nanoparticles on lemon peel carbon dots (LP-CDs@Fe3O4) applied in Photo-Catalysis, Antioxidant, Antidiabetic, and Hemolytic activity

coredata.dc:creator: Joga, Suri Babu
Korabandi, Deepthi
Lakkaboyana, Sivarama Krishna
Kumar, Vinay
coredata.dc:description: Herein, used a simple, economical hydrothermal method to produce the carbon quantum dots from leftover lemon peels (LP-CDs), and then it coated carbon quantum dots with phyto mediated iron magnetic nanoparticles (LP-CDs@Fe3O4). The synthesized materials were characterized through systematic approach. As synthesized, LP-CDs and LP-CDs@Fe3O4 were 3.52 and 15.9 nm in size, with coral, sphere-like shape and oxygen-rich surface functions identified through the TEM and SEM-EDX. The XRD investigation was approved as the crystalline nature of the LP-CD@Fe3O4 nanocomposites, with an average particle diameter of 18.12 nm. The optical band gap of pure LP-CDs and LP-CDs@ Fe3O4 are measured to be 1.9 eV and 1.5 eV, respectively. Methylene blue (MB) dye degradation was accomplished through the various types of optimization under natural sunshine irradiation. Data showed the superior dye degradation (98 %) in 1 min. Moreover, the nanocomposite (NC) demonstrated strong free radical scavenging activity (69.19 %), α-amylase inhibition (63.7 %), and hemolytic activity (1.44 %) at 1000 µg/mL. The little breakage of RBC membranes at 1000 µg/mL suggests modest haemolytic activity and probable safety for biomedical activities such as antioxidant, antidiabetic, and haemolytic characteristics, making them multifunctional materials suited for both environmental remediation and biomedical application.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Prof. Vinay Kumar is an eminent researcher in biomaterials, emerging environmental toxicants, and tissue engineering. Prof. Vinay Kumar completed his Ph.D. in biotechnology at the School of Biotechnology, Jawaharlal Nehru University, New Delhi, India, one of India's premier institutes. He worked as a postdoctoral Fellow in several countries overseas and served in industry as a Senior Scientist. In addition, he completed SERB research grant worth INR 2.25 million at the Indian Institute of Technology Roorkee. Currently, he is working as Professor and Head for the Biomaterials and Tissue Engineering (BITE) Laboratory, Department of Community Medicine, Saveetha Medical College, and Hospital, Saveetha Institute of Medical and Technical Sciences (SIMATS), Chennai, India. During his academic career, he has received several prestigious awards. He was awarded a Junior Research Fellowship (JRF) and a National Eligibility Test (NET) in Life Sciences award by CSIR, New Delhi, India. He was awarded a Junior Research Fellowship (JRF) in Biotechnology from the Department of Biotechnology, New Delhi, and Government of India. He was awarded the Graduate Aptitude Test in Engineering (GATE) in Life Science by IIT, India. He was awarded the National Eligibility Test (NET) in Agricultural Biotechnology by the Indian Council of Agricultural Research (ICAR), India. Moreover, he is a lifetime fellow of several reputed scientific societies. In addition, he has published several outstanding research papers in prestigious journals. Moreover, he has edited several book series by reputed publishers.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, used a simple, economical hydrothermal method to produce the carbon quantum dots from leftover lemon peels (LP-CDs), and then it coated carbon quantum dots with phyto mediated iron magnetic nanoparticles (LP-CDs@Fe3O4). The synthesized materials were characterized through systematic approach. As synthesized, LP-CDs and LP-CDs@Fe3O4 were 3.52 and 15.9 nm in size, with coral, sphere-like shape and oxygen-rich surface functions identified through the TEM and SEM-EDX. The XRD investigation was approved as the crystalline nature of the LP-CD@Fe3O4 nanocomposites, with an average particle diameter of 18.12 nm. The optical band gap of pure LP-CDs and LP-CDs@ Fe3O4 are measured to be 1.9 eV and 1.5 eV, respectively. Methylene blue (MB) dye degradation was accomplished through the various types of optimization under natural sunshine irradiation. Data showed the superior dye degradation (98 %) in 1 min. Moreover, the nanocomposite (NC) demonstrated strong free radical scavenging activity (69.19 %), α-amylase inhibition (63.7 %), and hemolytic activity (1.44 %) at 1000 µg/mL. The little breakage of RBC membranes at 1000 µg/mL suggests modest haemolytic activity and probable safety for biomedical activities such as antioxidant, antidiabetic, and haemolytic characteristics, making them multifunctional materials suited for both environmental remediation and biomedical application.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The textile industry, along with the printing, dyeing, and other manufacturing sectors, has been a key contributor to the global concern that has grown around wastewater management [1]. The existence of organic dyes like methylene blue (MB), which have complex aromatic compounds and are non-biodegradable, makes textile wastewater particularly troublesome since even at low concentrations, they can more impact onto human health and an environment [2]. This cationic dye that is widely utilized in biological, chemistry, medicine, and the dyeing industry. In aqueous solutions, it dissociates into cations and chloride anions [3]. The continuous exposure to the MB dye pretences a greater risk and can result in major health problems including hypertension, mental disorientation, methemoglobinemia, anaemia, skin discoloration, vomiting sensations, nausea, soreness, and extreme perspiration [4]. Thus researchers have used several methods to treat wastewater, such as filtration, photocatalysis, and adsorption using activated carbon. Among all of them, photo catalysis technique having more advantages and better one. Due to its great efficiency and affordability, photocatalysis has become one of these techniques that shows the most promise, especially in removing methylene blue (MB) [5]. This procedure employs a light source to generate electron hole pairs in the valence band (VB), and conduction band of the photocatalyst, thereby converting organic contaminants in wastewater convert to carbon dioxide, water, without producing secondary pollutants [5,7,8]. Furthermore, the effectiveness of photocatalysis is significantly influenced by the surface area and size of the catalyst. Nanomaterials, with their enhanced surface area due to the quantum size effect, exhibit improved capability in contaminant conversion, making them a promising solution for enhancing photocatalytic wastewater treatment performance [9].‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a low-dimensional nanocarbon family member that has attracted a lot of interest because of their fascinating chemical and physical characteristics [10]. Such as high water solubility, superior biocompatibility [11], chemical inertness, nontoxicity [13], photo-induced electron transfer, redox characteristics, up and down-converted photoluminescence, electron reservoir, and other characteristics are among them [12]. While single-walled carbon nanotubes were being synthesized, separated, and purified, CQDs were unintentionally discovered [13]. These zero-dimensional nanomaterials usually smaller than 10 nm. They are created by a variety of processes, including chemical oxidation [14], laser ablation [15], hydrothermal treatment [16], and microwave-assisted techniques [17]. The hydrothermal method is particularly well-known for its ease of use and environmental friendliness [18]. Nevertheless, problems like their tiny size, lack of polarity, dependence on external energy, and ability to produce toxic sludge’s limit their application, particularly in large-scale industrial settings. This inefficiency must be addressed with creative solutions that enhance the recovery and recycling of photocatalysts treated water.

According to reports, CQDs can create heterojunction to separate photogenerated charge carriers, increasing the photocatalytic activity of iron oxides and to easily recoverable from the treated water [16,19,20,21,22]. The qualities of magnetite include high magnetism, enormous surface area, low toxicity, and exceptional biocompatibility. By enhancing the photocatalyst's efficiency and facilitating the effective separation of treated water using an external magnetic field, these features greatly improve the water treatment process. Various studies have reported on the synthesis of Fe3O4 and carbon dots (C-dots) composites by a variety of methods, including stirring, reflux, sonication (physical attachment), and hydrothermal treatment [23]. For example, Fe2O3/C-dots composites have been successfully made by ultrasonicating Fe2O3 powder n-nitrobenzene and n-nitroaniline degradation that contains C-dots [22]. In another work, CDFe3O4 nanocomposites was produced through hydrothermal method and applied for photocatalysis of MB dye degradation under sunlight, it has degradation efficiency was 94.4 % in 60 min. It was claimed that the GS-Fe3O4/C-dots approached the ecologically friendly manufacturing of 10.1 nm-sized GS-Fe3O4/C-dots utilizing a hydrothermal technique; with this innovative method, the MB dye was impressively degradation by 98 % in just 30 min under UV exposure. Furthermore, with application of an external magnetic field, the GS-Fe3O4/C-dots demonstrated effective separate from the degraded solution [21].

Moreover, the novel form of fluorescence marker in nanobiotechnology, recently developed fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) or graphene nanosheets with tenable emission are generally regarded as having advantages over semiconductor QDs in terms of material toxicity and over traditional organic dyes in terms of optical brightness and photostability. Also, there are a number of ways to mass-produce CDs at a comparatively cheap cost [24]. However, due to the ability of these nanostructured materials such as gold, carbon nanotubes, and graphene’s to locally produce cytotoxic heat that kills cancer cells and strongly absorb near-infrared (NIR) light, nanomaterial-based photo thermal therapy (PTT) for treating solid tumours has also drawn a lot of attention [25,26]. A major obstacle is that total tumour ablation remains subtle due to the complexity of heat transfer in living things and the inadequacy of the local photo thermal cytotoxicity elicited by these nanoparticles to eradicate the spread of tumour cells. PTT in conjunction with chemotherapy is a preferable approach to increase treatment efficacy since chemotherapeutic drugs are more cytotoxic at higher temperatures [27].

The biomass-derived CQD surface's large supply of several functional oxygenic groups, such as hydroxyl, alkyl, epoxide, carboxyl, and carbonyl, helps explain their exceptional water-soluble properties [28]. The most important fruit in the world, citrus lemons, also referred to as lemons, are used for both culinary and non-culinary application [29]. Nevertheless, after the juice is extracted, the peels are usually thrown away and left to biodegrade, which could lead to dust formation and air pollution [30]. Consequently, it is crucial to investigate low-cost methods for turning lemon peel into goods with increased value.

Recently Jun Tae Kim et al. manufactured CQDs using lemon peel by hydrothermal method and it applied for food packing and biological properties [31]. Maitey et al. have been fabricated CODs using lemon peel by microwave method and applied for detection of tetracycline [32]. Toccata et al. also synthesized CODs by using lemon peels and it applied for communication devices [33]. Nevertheless, obtaining a large quantum yield is the limitation of lemon peel-derived CQDs reported in the literature. Compared to reported method our method is giving good yield of CQDS and good photo degradation of MB and good anti-oxidant and anti-diabatic properties.

Fe-NPs are oxidation-sensitive and agglomeration-prone, therefore surface shield, and passivation techniques were necessary to provide structural and colloidal stability over the long term [27,34]. Consequently, surfactants and macromolecules are chemically or physically adsorbed on Fe-NPs, and passivating layers composed of carbon, silica, and precious metals can accept a variety of functional group, enabling further conjugation with ligand, biomolecules, and nanostructure. A lot of research is being done on Fe-NPs in combination with peptides [35], DNA [36], aptamers [37], lipids [38], drug molecules, biopolymers [39], dendrimers [40], rare earth elements [41], MOF [42], GO [43,24], CNT [44], and fullerenes [45]. The production of composite based on Fe-NPs that have fluorescent characteristics due to the occurrence of carbon dots (C-dots), graphene quantum dots, and quantum dots, is given special attention [23]. C-dots' electrical and optical characteristics can be changed by heteroatom doping, which frequently results in the red-shifted emissive system [26]. Especially, hybrid nanomaterials made of C-dots and Fe-NPs (C/Fe-NPs) are excellent contenders for photothermal therapy, and optical bioimaging [18,27]. Dual-modal bio-imaging, bacteria sensing, and antimicrobial treatment, because they associate the superior magnetic assets of iron NPs with outstanding photo luminescent behaviour and the non-toxic nature of carbon dots. However, numerous innovative methodologies have been put forth for the production of the C/Fe-NPs, the construction of cost- and time-effective, ecologically safe [23].

Conventional procedures, while effective, have substantial limitations such as the use of toxic and costly reagents, the formation of damaging by-products, and the usage of chemical precursors. Reducing reliance on chemical precursors is necessary to meet these difficulties. As a result, a green synthesis method for creating Fe3O4@LPCD nanocomposites is presented in this work. The objective of this approach is to mitigate the effects on the environment while maintaining the effectiveness of the nanocomposites. Furthermore, the green-synthesized nanocomposites were evaluated to ensure their effectiveness for antimicrobial uses such as Antioxidant, Antidiabetic and Haemolytic assay.

Ferrous sulphate heptahydrate-99 % (FeSO4⋅7H2O), Ferric chloride hexahydrate-98.5 % (FeCl3·9H2O), Hydrogen peroxide (35 % w/w) NaOH and Methylene Blue dye (MB), were procured from Fluka, India. Lemon peels were obtained from local Bhimavaram, Andhra Pradesh, India fruit market.

The lemon fruits were gathered from the nearby Bhimavaram, Andhra Pradesh, India fruit market. The lemons were washed with distilled water for 2 times, the obtained peels were allowed to dry in the sunshade. After drying, the peels were ground into a fine powder. To make the extract, 100 ml DDW, and 5 g of lemon peel powder were combined in a 250 ml Erlenmeyer flask. The mixture was rapidly agitated at 80 °C on a hot plate to extract the bioactive components. Finally, the combination was subsequently filtered via filter paper, and the resultant extract was stored in the fridge for further use.

In this step, first transfer 80 ml of the lemon peel extract into a 100 ml Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave then seal the autoclave tightly and transfer into the hot air oven maintained 180 °C for 3 h. After the reaction is complete, to allow the autoclave to cool naturally. After that filter the reaction mixture using Whatman No. 1 filter paper to remove large particles. The obtained solution is taken into centrifuge tubes and centrifuge at 10,000 rpm for 20 min. Further, the filter the dark brown solution using a 0.22 µm syringe filter. The finally obtained carbon dots transferred in to a clean, airtight container, away from light and moisture and stored at 4 °C, until further uses.

In this phase, freshly prepared solutions of 0.2 M ferric chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O) and 0.1 M ferrous sulphate heptahydrate (FeSO4·7H2O) were dissolved in distilled water in separate beakers. These solutions were mixed in a 1:1 M ratio in a clean beaker, to this 25 mL of previously prepared lemon peel extract was added drop by drop with vigorous stirring at room temperature for 30 min. The pH of the mixed solution was adjusted to 11 by using a freshly prepared 1 M sodium hydroxide (NaOH) solution. After that stirring was continued until a dark brown precipitate formed. Then solution was allowed to settle after the stirring was stopped when a dark brown precipitated. The attained precipitate was filtered using Whatman No. 1 filter paper and washed with distilled water and ethanol successively. The Fe3O4 nanoparticles were collected and dried at 90 °C for 3 h.

The process of fabrication of the CD@Fe3O4 nanocomposites is shown in Fig. 1 . First, 0.8 g of Fe3O4 nanoparticles were added to 10 ml of CD solution. The reaction mixture was mixed at room temperature using ultrasonic waves (500 W, 40 kHz) for 30 min, after that nanocomposites were obtained by centrifuging the reaction mixture at 9000 rpm for 15 min. The product was washed several times with deionized water and then dried at 250 °C for 24 h, resulting in CDs being decorated on the surface of Fe3O4.

The UV–visible absorbance spectra were recorded using an LKB BIOCHROM 4050/4051 spectrophotometer. HR-TEM images were captured using a PHILIPS CM120 transmission electron microscope. FTIR spectra were obtained at room temperature using an IRAffinity-1 SHIMADZU spectrometer. The SEM image was acquired using a TESCAN MIRA III scanning electron microscope (SEM), which is equipped with an EDX system for energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis. EDX analysis was performed with an accelerated voltage of 15 kV on freeze-dried carbon dots attached to the sample holder. The sample was coated with an extremely thin layer of gold using the TESCAN MIRA III SEM coating system. High-energy electrons were focused on the sample to generate signals. The XRD pattern was obtained using a Philips PW 1730 instrument.

To investigate the photo degradation of methylene blue (MB) solutions was performed under natural sunlight. The 10 mg of the CD@Fe3O4 composite was utilized as the photocatalyst, was added to 100 ml of 10 ppm MB solution and 0.042 M H2O2 in a beaker. To enhance dye adsorption on the photocatalyst surface and ensure proper dispersion and equilibrium of adsorption–desorption processes between the catalyst surface and MB, the mixture was kept in the dark for 30 min. Subsequently, the reaction mixture was exposed to natural light at midday with continuous stirring. Samples of 5 mL were taken from the reaction solution every 10 min, and the photocatalyst was separated using a permanent magnet. The concentration of MB (λmax = 664 nm) in the final solution was determined using UV–Vis spectroscopy. The percentage of dye degradation (η) was calculated using Equation (1).
(1)

Co - C e

× 100
Here C0 and Ce are initial and final concentration after adsorption or photo degradation respectively. The catalyst dose (10 mg to 100 mg), various concentration of H2O2 dose (0.3 ml to 0.9 ml of a 0.045 M solution) resulting in different concentration of peroxide (0.03–0.08 mM), different concentration of MB dye, and different pH (7–12) were varied in order to find the optimum conditions and evaluate the effect of each parameter on decolouration efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural characterization of LP-CDs and LP-CDs@Fe3O4 nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: The spectral analysis was carried out to further LP-CD and CD@Fe3O4 nanocomposites. The Fig. 2 a. and 2b. display the UV–Vis absorption spectrum. The absorption peak at 267 nm and 320 nm are exhibit the main prominent in LP-CD. These are attributed to the π-π* transitions of aromatic sp2 domains and n-π* transitions of surface functional groups, respectively. Which is consistent with previous results for graphitic nano carbons. The inset of Fig. 2(a) shows that the LP-CDs aqueous solution showed intense blue luminescence when exposed to UV light. The LP-CDs are highly soluble in water, which leads to a highly distributed solution. In contrast, the LP-CDs@Fe3O4 nanocomposites display a broad absorption band spanning 274–290 nm and an additional sharp peak at 194 nm (Fig. 2b). The broad peak is indicative of the complex interaction between LP-CDs and Fe3O4 nanoparticles, likely resulting from the synergistic electronic coupling and charge transfer between the carbonaceous matrix and the magnetic Fe3O4. The sharp peak at 194 nm is characteristic of Fe3O4, signifying its intrinsic electronic transitions. This significant shift and broadening of absorption features in LP-CDs@Fe3O4 nanocomposites the enhanced optical properties and potential multifunctional applications of these nanohybrids in fields such as photocatalysis, bioimaging, and drug delivery. Due to metal-to-graphite charge-transfer transitions, LP-CDs@Fe3O4 exhibited higher absorption intensity from the visible to the near-infrared spectrum as compared to CDs [45].

From the UV–vis DRS spectra, the Tauc plot was drawn between (αhv)2 and hv. It was shown in Fig. 2c & 2d. The band gaps of LP-CDs and LP-CDs@Fe3O4 were found to be 1.9 eV and 1.5 eV, respectively. The reduced band gap in LP-CDs@Fe3O4 enables absorption of lower-energy photons, boosting electron-hole pair generation. These combined with enhanced light absorption, efficient charge separation, and increased reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation, results in superior photocatalytic performance of LP-CDs@Fe3O4 compared to LP-CDs alone [46].

The Fig. 2e & 2f denoted the Fourier transform infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) analysis of LP-CDs and LP-CDs@Fe3O4 nanocomposites. The FTIR spectrum of LP-CDs exhibited prominent peaks at 677, 1042, 1216, 1365, 1580, and 2926 cm−1, corresponding to C–H bending, C-O stretching, C-N stretching, C–H deformation, C = C stretching, and C–H stretching vibrations, respectively. It can indicating the presence of various functional groups integral to LP-CDs. In contrast, the LP-CDs@Fe3O4 nanocomposites revealed peaks at 596, 670, 1070, 1404, 1621, and 2924 cm−1. The peak at 596 cm−1 is indicative of Fe-O stretching, confirming the incorporation of Fe3O4 into the carbon dot matrix [19,20,21]. The absorption at 670 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibrations of C–H bonds, similar to the carbon dots. The band at 1070 cm−1, associated with C-O stretching vibrations, indicates the retention of functional groups from the carbon dots. The peaks at 1404 and 1621 cm−1 can be attributed to the bending vibrations of C–H bonds and C = C stretching vibrations, respectively, suggesting the integration of carbon dots within the Fe3O4 matrix. The presence of a peak at 2924 cm−1, associated with aliphatic C–H stretching vibrations [13], further confirms the incorporation of carbon dots into the nanocomposites. These FTIR results collectively confirm the successful synthesis and structural integrity of LP-CDs@Fe3O4 nanocomposites, demonstrating the retention of key functional groups from both carbon dots and nanocomposites.

Fig. 2g & 2h display the XRD patterns of LP-CDs and LP-CDs@Fe3O4 nanocomposites. The CD exhibited a broad diffraction peak at approximately 2θ = 28°, approve that amorphous structure of carbon material. Upon integration with Fe3O4, the XRD pattern of the magnetic nanocomposite retained a characteristic peak for CD at 2θ = 21° that is clearly related to the amorphous nature of C-dots [13], while introducing distinct diffraction peaks corresponding to Fe3O4 at 2θ values of 30.12°, 32.71°, 35.7°, 57.12°, and 63.05° [15,19,21]. These additional peaks confirm the successful formation of the LP-CDs@Fe3O4 nanocomposite, showcasing the crystalline nature of the Fe3O4 component within the composite matrix. Furthermore, applying the Scherer equation revealed that LP-CDs have an average crystalline size of 2.3 nm at a diffraction angle of 2θ = 28.28°, while the LP-CDs@Fe3O4 nanocomposites exhibit a significantly larger average crystal size is 18.12 nm. This results demonstrating the successful incorporation and growth of Fe3O4 on the LP-CDs.

Fig. 3 a-f shows typical transmission electron microscopy (TEM) pictures and their histogram of LP-CDs and LP-CDs@Fe3O4 NCs, respectively. Fig. 3a of LP-CDs displaying the clear sphere-like morphology with an average size of 3.52 nm. The histogram figure illustrates the average of the measured diameters of dried hybrid CDs (Fig. 3b).

Fig. 3 c & d shows the TEM image of LP-CDs@Fe3O4 NCs and it histogram, Fig. 3 c reveals that spherical shape and it average size is around 15.9 nm (Fig. 3d). It compared to the bare CDs size was increased. This increment of size completely approved the LP-CDs@Fe3O4 NCs formation conformation. From Fig. 3e the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) of LP-CD manifesting the LP-CDs polycrystal nature [47] and Fig. 3f depicts the SAED pattern of NCs showing the characteristic circular diffraction patterns corresponding to (002), (220), (221), (311), (511) and (440) planes of cubic phase of Fe3O4. It was evident with previous literature [48].

Fig. 4 a and 4b Show the FESEM measurements of LP-CDs and LP-CDs@Fe3O4 NCs were indicting the coral and spherical shaped nanoparticle are formed in the samples. Energy dispersive analysis of X-rays was carried out to further validate the composition of the LP-CDs and LP-CDs@Fe3O4 nanocomposites. The energy dispersive spectrum (EDS) of Fig. 4 a-d display surface morphology FESEM and SEM-EXD. The Fig. 4c indicating the formation of LP-CDs purely without any interference. The pure LP-CDs spectrum consists of only C (44.94%), N (5.09%), O (49.96%) elements. The Fe3O4 coated LP-CDs showing the C (10.24%), O (31.86%) and Fe (57.88%) elements (Fig. 4d). The magnetic iron oxide nanocrystals in the carbon shell and core are responsible for the Fe, O, and C. The Fe element appearing and showing highest percentage in the LP-CDs@Fe3O4 NCs. It is clearly is conformation of the formation of nanocomposite. After combining all of these analytical data, it is reasonable to assume that the LP-CDs@Fe3O4 NCs consist of fluorescent CDs embedded in the carbon network shell and iron oxide nanocrystals in the core.

The different amount of photocatalyst (10 mg, 50 mg, 100 mg) was utilized and performed photocatalytic reaction under sunlight in the presence of 0.9 ml H2O2 (0.045 M), and the results are shown in Fig. 5 . The Fig. 5 revels the adsorption–desorption equilibrium percentage was increased from 15.25 %, to 45.85 % (10––100 mg). After expose to natural sunlight the degradation percentage remarkably increased up to 99.24 % within 60 sec for 50 mg dose (Fig. 5c) and the degradation reached 99.56 % within just 30 sec for 100 mg dose (Fig. 5d). For the 10 mg concentration, 98.12 % degradation was observed within 60 s (Fig. 5b), as confirmed by UV–visible spectroscopy. The rapid degradation observed (Fig. 5a), particularly at higher catalyst dose amount, can be attributed to the increased availability of active sites on the nanocomposites. The formation of electron-hole pairs is impeded, and H2O2 has fewer active sites available for reacting and producing the radicals. It can be enhancing the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) when exposed to sunlight. Moreover, LP-CDs@Fe3O4 nanocomposites synergistic impact with H2O2 promotes efficient breakdown of MB molecules, in particularly, has a low band gap, which is important because it facilitates its reaction with visible light. The results are approved to increasing the catalyst amount increasing in MB degradation.

The pH parameter is led key role in photo catalysis/adsorption processes. The solid evidence suggests that solution pH has significant impact on oxidant solubility and activity, reaction kinetics, radical production, catalyst surface characteristics, and pollutant levels. In this study, the degradation of MB dye was catalysed by LP-CDs@Fe3O4 NCs under the natural sunlight has been shown to be highly effective, with performance strongly influenced by pH of solution. The Fig. 6 depicts the effects of natural and alkaline environment such as pHs 7, 9, 10, 11, and 12. The influence of pH study was examining degradation efficiency over one hour at pH 7–12, the obtained degradation percentage was shown in Table 1 . It was observed that without any pH adjustment, the degradation efficiency was only 3.46 % (Fig. 6 a). These results approved with the biosynthesized α-Fe2O3 nanoparticles shows the degradation of 10 % at pH 3 [49]. However, as the pH increased, the degradation efficiency significantly improved. At neutral pH 7, the degradation reached 32.67 %. This efficiency increased significantly in alkaline conditions, with degradation percentages of 75.80 %, 95.18 %, 97.27, and 99.09 % at pH 9, 10, 11, and 12, respectively (Fig. 6b). These results that the photo catalytic activity of LP-CDs@Fe3O4 NCs is completely pH-dependent, with alkaline conditions promoting the fast degradation processes. The enhanced performance in higher pH environments can be attributed to the increased availability of hydroxyl ions, which are essential for creating reactive hydroxyl radical via photo catalytic processes. These free radicals are acting as powerful oxidizing agents, it can be effectively break down the MB dye molecules. Therefore, optimizing the pH to alkaline level is crucial to maximize the degradation efficiency of MB utilizing LP-CDs@Fe3O4 NCs under natural sunlight (Fig. 6c).

The influence of H2O2 dosage on MB dye degradation was investigated, as shown in Fig. 7 . The quantity of H2O2 amount ranged from 0.3 mL, 0.6 mL, and 0.9 mL of a 0.045 M solution, the degradation efficiency was assessed over different time intervals, showing significant variance dependent on the H2O2 concentration. The degradation was increased with increasing the amount of H2O2. This is mostly happened due to the development of more radicals of ·OH with raising of H2O2 dose. Notably, a 90.08 % degradation of MB was achieved within 120 min with 0.3 ml of 0.045 M H2O2 (Fig. 7a). Remarkable results were observed with higher concentrations, where 98.5 % degradation occurred within 180 s for 0.6 ml of 0.045 M H2O2 (Fig. 7b), and an impressive 98.12 % degradation was realized in just 60 s with 0.9 ml of 0.045 M H2O2 (Fig. 7c). These results indicate that increasing the concentration of H2O2 significantly enhances the degradation rate of MB dye, demonstrating the efficacy of LP-CDs@Fe3O4 NCs as a photocatalyst in the presence of natural sunlight in the presence of H2O2.

The impact of starting dye concentration varied from 10 to 50 ppm on the decolourization of MB dye by sunlight processes was examined with a H2O2 concentration 0.042 M (0.9 ml) with 10 mg of catalyst. The various concentration effect degradation was shown in Fig. 8 and their percentage was tabulated in Table 2 . The Fig. 8 a) revels that the complete degradation (100 %) of 50 ppm MB dye was achieved within 210 min. In contrast, a 97.15 % degradation of 25 ppm MB dye was observed within 90 min under the same conditions (Fig. 8 b). The comparison indicates that the degradation of lower concentration dye (25 ppm) occurs more rapidly than higher concentration dye (50 ppm) [19]. This suggests that for applications requiring quick dye degradation, lower initial dye concentrations are more favourable. Furthermore, at 10 ppm dye concentration with the same condition (Fig. 8 c), the rate of degradation would likely be even faster, illustrating that dye concentration has a significant impact on the efficiency and speed of the degradation process (Fib. 8d). The main reason behind it was the limited generation of radicles, which were insufficient for the degradation of a high concentration of MB dye. Furthermore, a large concentration of MB limits the photocatalytic interaction by limiting the light penetration, reducing electron/hole pair photogeneration of high contrition of MB dye. Additionally, the active sites on the surface of the LP-CDs@Fe3O4 NCs photocatalyst may be occupied by the adsorbed dye molecules, it can led to the affecting the photogeneration processes [50].

In a ground breaking experiment, the degradation of methylene blue (MB) dye was investigated using LP-CDs@Fe3O4 NCs as a photocatalyst under natural sunlight. The experiment, conducted at a dye concentration of 10 ppm, using with a H2O2 concentration 0.042 M (0.9 ml) and a pH of 10 (Fig. 9 a to 9c), revealed exceptional performance, with a rapid degradation of 85.75 % in just 0.2 min, reaching 99.40 % degradation in only 3 min (Fig. 9b). This remarkable efficiency can be attributed to the synergistic action of the LP-CDs and Fe3O4. The LP-CDs, with their excellent light absorption properties, harnessed the wide solar spectrum, while Fe3O4 enhanced the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), expediting dye breakdown. The high pH (10) further boosted the process by increasing hydroxyl radical (•OH) formation, which played a crucial role in degrading the dye (Fig. 9c). With a swift reaction time and near-total removal, the LP-CDs@Fe3O4 NCs photocatalyst proved to be a powerful tool for MB dye degradation, offering great promise for efficient wastewater treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS)
ce:section.ce:para:
The degradation of MB dye using LP-CDs@Fe3O4 catalyst under the natural sum light reaction was performed and drawn the mechanism it was shown in the Fig. 10 . Once catalyst was adsorbed the photons, it caused generating electron-hole pairs within the conduction band and valence band (Equ.1). The formation of these charges eventually assistances in the production of reactive species (ROS), which may readily destroy the dye molecules. The photogenerated electrons in CB may react with dissolved oxygen to form superoxide radicals (O2 −) through the reduction reaction (Eq.3), while the holes (h+) in the valence band oxidize water to producing the hydroxyl radicals (•OH) (Eq. (3) [51,52,53,6]. Additionally, the Fe3O4 component further activates hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) through a Fenton-like reaction, generating additional hydroxyl radicals (Equ.5). The Fe3+ ions are reduced back to Fe2+ by the photo-generated electrons (Eq.5), continuing the ROS generation cycle [54]. The generated ROS primarily consist of hydroxyl radicals (•OH) and superoxide radicals (O2 −), which attack methylene blue dye molecules, breaking them down into smaller, less harmful molecules (Equ.7) [55,56,57,58].
(2) CD/Fe3O4 + hv → CD/Fe3O4 (e-+h+)
(3) O2 + e-→O2 –
(4) H2O + h+→ . OH + H+
(5) Fe+2 + H2O2 → Fe+3+ . OH + OH–
(6) Fe+3 + e - →Fe+2
(7) MB dye + . OH → Degraded products

The LP-CDs@Fe3O4 is also checked for recyclability. After completion of the reaction, the NC was centrifuged and dried at 70 °C for 4 h. Later the NC was used for dye degradation it took 80 sec for the complete degradation of the MB at 98.5 %. The catalyst is centrifuged and dried at 70 °C for 4 h. Again, the NC was used for degradation study under sunlight it effectively degraded at 97.8 % for 100 sec, and the time increase and percentage increased due to the loss of the catalyst during the recycling process. Finally, it can be recycled up to 6 cycles without loss of significant catalytic activity. The obtained results are presented in Fig. 11 .

Finally, the synthesized LP-CDs@Fe3O4 catalyst was compared with reported methods and the results are presented in Table 3 . Recently, Edakkaparamban et al. have synthesized CQDS from coconut husks (UCQDS/NCQDS) and applied it for dye degradation of MB it takes 180 min time and the removal efficiency was 94 % [60]. On the other hand, Gea and his co-workers reported N‑Doped ZnO/Carbon Dots by using soybean and its degradation takes place 83.4 % and dye concentration is 10 ppm only [61]. In 2023 M. Ahmed and his group have been reported CQDS form chicken feather and it applied for MB dye degradation takes 92 % for 1 h [62]. El-Shamy and Zayed prepared CQDS form glucose and it make composite with poly vinyl alcohol (PVA) it showed 97 % about 40 min time [63]. Palanisamy et al. have been reported CQDS supported zinc oxide (ZnO) for degradation of MB it showed 96 % for 30 min [64]. Compared to among all these reported methods on MB, our method showed very good results about 99.4 % within minutes.

In this study, the antioxidant properties of LP-CDs@Fe3O4 nanocomposite were evaluated using the 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) radical scavenging technique. The experimental procedure involved incubating 1 mL of a 0.1 mM DPPH solution with various concentrations (10, 50, 100, 300, 500, 700 and 1000 µg/mL) of the LP-CDs@Fe3O4 nanocomposite at 37 °C for 1 h under dark conditions. The positive control used ascorbic acid (50 mM), with the negative control being the DPPH solution alone. After incubation, each sample’s absorbance was measured at 517 nm using a microplate reader. The radical scavenging activity (RSA) was calculated using the following formula:
(8)
Scavengingpercentage
( % )
=
( A o - A 1 )
Ao
X 100
here Ao represents the absorbance of the control, and A1 denotes the absorbance of the sample.

Generally, DPPH is a stable free radical that is commonly used to analyse the radical scavenging activity of freshly synthesized compounds as well as their antioxidant properties in food and biological structures. The DPPH is mixed with the substance that may donate hydrogen of electron radicals, it creates a stable diamagnetic species and discoloration from yellow to purple. The adsorption peak at 517 decreased. The present study shows the interaction of the DPPH free radical with LP-CDs@Fe3O4 nanocomposite leads to a gradual shift in the colour of DPPH from yellow to purple. This allows for visual monitoring of the antioxidant activity of the carrier. Fig. 12 a, b shows the radical scavenging activity ofthe LP-CDs@Fe3O4 is DPPH assay, and different doses compared to normal ascorbic acid (as a standard antioxidant).

The synthesized LP-CDs@Fe3O4 nanocomposite exhibited 2.1, 4.85, 8.14, 14.31, 31.24, 63.77, and 69.19 percent free radical scavenging activity at concentrations of 10, 50, 100, 300, 500, and 1000 µg/mL, respectively. The results demonstrated that the sample’s DPPH scavenging activity enhanced in a dose-dependent way. The increased antioxidant properties of the LP-CDs@Fe3O4 nanocomposites are attributed to the synergistic interaction between the carbon dots and Fe3O4 nanoparticles, which enhances electron transfer and neutralization of DPPH radicals. Moreover, the LP-CDs@Fe3O4 show high antioxidant activity due to hydroxyl groups. The reduces the enthalpy of O–H bond dissociation and enhances the rate of H-atom transfer to proxy radical. The LP-CDs@Fe3O4 cationic free radical is produced by interacting with a strong oxidizing agent and may be neutralized by strong antioxidant molecule. The color change in each antioxidant activity is determined by the intensity and concentration of the antioxidant molecules [41,47,59].

The inhibitory effect of LP-CDs@Fe3O4 nanocomposite on α-amylase activity was evaluated using the α-amylase inhibition assay. Various concentrations of LP-CDs@Fe3O4 (10, 50, 100, 300, 500, 700, and 1000 µg/mL) were prepared and mixed with α-amylase enzyme and phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) buffer (pH = 6.8), followed by incubation at 37 °C for 10 min. Subsequently, a 0.5 % starch solution was added to each mixture and further incubated for 15 min at 37 °C. The reaction was terminated by adding 3,5-dinitrosalicylic acid (DNSA) reagent, and the mixtures were heated in boiling water for 5 min. After cooling to room temperature and dilution, the absorbance of each solution was measured at 540 nm using an ELISA plate reader. Acarbose, a known α-amylase inhibitor, was used as the positive control. The obtained results were shown in Fig. 13 a & b.

The percentage of α-amylase inhibition was assessed for each concentration. The results revels that the controller exhibited an inhibition percentage of 0 %, while the LP-CDs@Fe3O4 nanocomposites achieved a significantly higher inhibition percentage of 63.7 % (1000 µg/mL) (Fig. 13b).

A recent study found that bio-based NPs had effective anti-diabetic properties, with FeNPs inhibiting α-glycosidase by 58.37 % [65,66]. LP-CDs@Fe3O4 nanocomposites was enhanced antidiabetic properties due to their increased binding sites and the synergistic interactions between carbon dots and Fe3O4 nanoparticles, which amplify α-amylase inhibition [67]. Additionally, their unique structural and magnetic properties contribute to improved stability and reactivity, making them highly effective in slowing down starch breakdown and reducing postprandial blood glucose levels. Moreover, LP extract contain polyphenols and flavonoids, terpenoids and other compounds were responsible for the lower blood glucose levels. The majority of the antidiabetic effects of medicinal plants are function to controlling the glycaemic metabolism, lower cholesterol levels, removing free radicals, increasing insulin production and improving microcirculation [68].

Assessing hemolytic activity is crucial for determining the biocompatibility of nanomaterials in biomedical applications, it was shown in Fig. 14 a & b. In this study, we evaluated the hemolytic potential of LP-CDs@Fe3O4 nanocomposites using red blood cells (RBCs) as a model. Hemolysis was quantified by measuring the percentage of hemoglobin released from RBCs upon exposure to the nanocomposites.

The collected blood was centrifuged for 20 min at 2000 rpm, and the supernatant was removed. The pellet was washed three times with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, pH 7.4) and resuspended in PBS. This RBC suspension was then mixed with varying concentrations of LP-CDs@Fe3O4 nanocomposites (10, 50, 100, 300, 500, 700, and 1000 µg/mL). RBCs treated with Triton-X 100 served as the positive control, while RBCs along withPBS alone were used as the negative control. After incubating the mixtures for one hour at 37 °C and centrifuging them for five minutes at 5000 rpm, we recorded the absorbance of the supernatant at 540 nm to calculate the percentage of hemolysis by the following equation
(9)
Hemolysis =
A ( s a m p l e ) - A ( n e g a t i v e
c o n t r o l )
A ( p o s i t i v e
c o n t r o l ) - A ( n e g a t i v e
c o n t r o l )
X 100
where, As is the absorbance of the sample, A0 is the absorbance of negative control and A+ is the absorbance of positive control

RBCs were exposed to LP-CDs@Fe3O4 nanocomposites at a concentration of 1000 µg/mL. Hemolytic activity was compared against a positive control (RBCs with Triton-X 100) and a negative control (RBCs with PBS). The nanocomposites induced a hemolysis rate of 1.44 %, indicating minimal disruption of RBC membranes. The positive control exhibited complete hemolysis, validating the assay's sensitivity, while the negative control showed negligible hemolysis, confirming the RBCs' baseline integrity. These findings suggest that LP-CDs@Fe3O4 nanocomposites have low hemolytic activity, indicating their potential safety for biomedical applications involving blood contact.

The LP-CDs were synthesized economically and the pytomediated Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles productively coated on LP-CDs in simple processes. The FTIR, UV, XRD, SEM-EDX, and TEM analysis confirmed the formation of LP-CDs@ Fe3O4 NC successfully. The prepared LP-CDs and LP-CDs@ Fe3O4 were confirmed sphere-like shape with 18.12 nm, with having oxygen-rich surface. The band gap was developed with coating of iron nanomaterial on LP-CDs, it was reduce from 1.9 to 1.5 eV. Due this low band gap the LP-CDs@ Fe3O4 NC was showing excellent photo degradation of MB in within mint (98 %). Additionally, the LP-CDs@ Fe3O4 NC showing the strong free radical scavenging activity, α-amylase inhibition activity and modest RBC breakage activity. Subsequently, the LP-CDs@ Fe3O4 NC catalyst has a promising future for use in commercial wastewater treatment applications as well as biological application.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Mr. Suri Babu Joga is working as an Assistant Professor at the Department of Chemistry, Vishnu Institute of Technology, Bhimavaram, India, and a Research Scholar at Department of Chemistry, Adikavi Nannya University, Rajamundry, India. He has 12 years of teaching experience. His areas of research interest include Green synthesis, photocatalysis, Environmental Applications, and nanotechnology. He has qualified for the APSET-2020 State-level Eligibility Test as an Assistant Professor/ Lecturer.

Dr. Deepthi Korabandi working as an Assistant Professor at the Department of Chemistry, Adikavi Nannaya University, Rajahmundry, Andhra Pradesh, India. She has completed her PhD from Andhra University, Visakhapatnam. Research areas of interest are a synthesis of Nanomaterial’s using various novel methods and their applications. She has published about 11 peer-reviewed research articles in international journals with 1 patent publication.

Dr. Sivarama Krishna Lakkaboyana Mailing Address is currently working as an Associate Professor (Research) at Nanomaterial’s Laboratory, Dept. of Chemistry, VelTech University, and Chennai, India. He has about 12 years of experience in the research and academic sectors. He was awarded the Graduate Aptitude Test in Engineering (GATE) in Chemical Science by IIT (Kanpur), India. He has maintained an exemplary academic record throughout his career, with significant research experience in fields such as Wastewater Treatment, Green Synthesis of Nanomaterials, Nanotechnology, Biological Activity, and Biomedical Research. He has published more than 65 peer-reviewed papers in reputable national and international journals, along with 6 book chapters. He has edited two book series by reputed publishers and acted as a reviver for Springer, Science Direct, RSC, ACS, MDPI, Wiley, and Bentham publishers. His research contributions have garnered over 1906 citations, an h-index of 22, and an i10-index of 39, with a cumulative impact factor exceeding 230, apart from 2 patents published to his credit (One UK and One German). His scientific expertise has earned him prestigious awards, such as the Senior Postdoctoral Fellowship from the Chulalongkorn University (Thailand), Post-Doctoral Fellowship from Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia (Malaysia), Universiti Teknologi Malaysia (Malaysia), Visiting Post-Doctoral Researcher from Ton Duc Thang University (Vietnam), and Visiting Researcher Fellow from Universiti Teknologi Malaysia (Malaysia). Previously, he served as an Assistant Professor at Universiti Malaysia Terengganu, Malaysia, and Dr.K.V.SRIT, Kurnool, India. : Nanomaterial’s Lab, Room NO: 29017, Research Park, VelTech University, Chennai-600065, India.-mail: svurams@gail.com

Prof. Vinay Kumar is an eminent researcher in biomaterials, emerging environmental toxicants, and tissue engineering. Prof. Vinay Kumar completed his Ph.D. in biotechnology at the School of Biotechnology, Jawaharlal Nehru University, New Delhi, India, one of India's premier institutes. He worked as a postdoctoral Fellow in several countries overseas and served in industry as a Senior Scientist. In addition, he completed SERB research grant worth INR 2.25 million at the Indian Institute of Technology Roorkee. Currently, he is working as Professor and Head for the Biomaterials and Tissue Engineering (BITE) Laboratory, Department of Community Medicine, Saveetha Medical College, and Hospital, Saveetha Institute of Medical and Technical Sciences (SIMATS), Chennai, India. During his academic career, he has received several prestigious awards. He was awarded a Junior Research Fellowship (JRF) and a National Eligibility Test (NET) in Life Sciences award by CSIR, New Delhi, India. He was awarded a Junior Research Fellowship (JRF) in Biotechnology from the Department of Biotechnology, New Delhi, and Government of India. He was awarded the Graduate Aptitude Test in Engineering (GATE) in Life Science by IIT, India. He was awarded the National Eligibility Test (NET) in Agricultural Biotechnology by the Indian Council of Agricultural Research (ICAR), India. Moreover, he is a lifetime fellow of several reputed scientific societies. In addition, he has published several outstanding research papers in prestigious journals. Moreover, he has edited several book series by reputed publishers.
